questions.question: Define Computer Vision and explain its primary goal.
keyPoints: Computer Vision enables machines to interpret visual information
It involves acquiring, processing, analyzing, and understanding digital images
Primary goal is to replicate human vision capabilities in machines
Produces numerical or symbolic information from visual data

questions.question: Explain the relationship between Image Processing and Computer Vision.
keyPoints: Image Processing is a subset of Computer Vision
Image Processing focuses on enhancing or transforming images
Computer Vision aims to extract meaningful semantic information
Image Processing deals with input-to-output transformation while CV deals with understanding

questions.question: Discuss three major applications of Computer Vision in modern technology.
keyPoints: Facial recognition for security and authentication
Autonomous vehicles for navigation and obstacle detection
Medical image analysis for diagnosis and treatment
Other applications: surveillance, AR/VR, quality control in manufacturing

questions.question: What is the significance of the Block World experiment in Computer Vision history?
keyPoints: Conducted by Larry Roberts in 1963
One of the earliest Computer Vision systems
Could recognize simple geometric shapes from 2D images
Pioneered the approach of interpreting complex scenes from simple components

questions.question: Explain David Marr's contribution to Computer Vision.
keyPoints: Proposed computational theory of vision in 1970s
Introduced hierarchical processing concept in visual perception
Influential framework for understanding visual information processing
His work remains foundational in computational vision research

questions.question: What fields intersect to form Computer Vision? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Artificial Intelligence - provides learning and reasoning frameworks
Machine Learning - enables pattern recognition from data
Mathematics - provides geometry and optimization tools
Physics - understanding light and optics
Neuroscience - insights from biological vision systems
Computer Science - provides computational frameworks

questions.question: What was the impact of the 2012 AlexNet breakthrough on Computer Vision?
keyPoints: AlexNet won the ImageNet competition in 2012
Demonstrated power of deep learning for image classification
Sparked the deep learning revolution in CV
Led to rapid advances in object detection, segmentation, and other CV tasks

questions.question: Trace the historical evolution of Computer Vision from its origins to the modern deep learning era. Discuss key milestones and pioneers.
keyPoints: 1960s: Origins with Block World experiment (Larry Roberts)
1970s: David Marr's computational theory of vision
1980s: First CV summer school (CMU 1982), feature-based approaches
1990s: Statistical learning methods, face detection advances
2000s: Large-scale datasets, real-world applications
2012+: Deep learning revolution with CNNs
Key pioneers: Roberts, Marr, Kanade, and others

questions.question: Explain the multidisciplinary nature of Computer Vision. How do various fields contribute to its development?
keyPoints: AI provides learning and reasoning frameworks
Machine Learning enables automatic pattern recognition
Mathematics supplies geometry, algebra, optimization
Physics explains light, color, and optical phenomena
Neuroscience provides models from biological vision
Computer Science provides algorithms and computational power
The intersection creates powerful visual understanding systems

questions.question: What are the current challenges in Computer Vision? Discuss at least five challenges and their implications.
keyPoints: Robustness in varying conditions (lighting, angles, occlusions)
Understanding context and 3D geometry from 2D images
Real-time processing requirements for applications
Achieving human-level understanding and reasoning
Bias and fairness in visual recognition systems
Data efficiency and generalization to new scenarios
Ethical considerations and privacy concerns

questions.question: Discuss the difference between Computer Vision and human vision. How do these differences impact the design of CV systems?
keyPoints: Humans process continuous signals vs computers process discrete pixels
Humans have biological limitations vs cameras have sensor limitations
Humans understand context instinctively vs CV requires explicit modeling
Computers can scale processing but lack common sense understanding
CV systems need to handle variations that humans find trivial
Design implications: feature engineering, data augmentation, robustness
